content.text: Kotlin coroutines offer two primitives for moving values between coroutines:

. They have different backpressure semantics, lifetimes, and sharing behaviour. For Claude SSE streams the distinction is practical: the OkHttp callback runs on a dispatcher thread, the UI reads on the main thread, and tokens must be delivered in order without dropping. Choosing the wrong primitive — or the wrong backpressure strategy — results in lost tokens, leaked coroutines, or UI thread contention.

attrs.alt: Channel vs Flow comparison, backpressure strategies (sequential, buffer, conflate, collectLatest), and callbackFlow bridge from OkHttp SSE to ViewModel StateFlow
attrs.title: Channels vs Flow Backpressure for Claude Streams

(a non-blocking CAS operation), the default is ideal. The other strategies exist for slow consumers:

Adds a buffered channel between the producer and consumer, letting the producer run ahead while the consumer processes at its own pace. Appropriate only when the collect block is CPU-heavy (e.g. Markdown parsing on each token):
Do not use for Claude token accumulation.
drops intermediate emissions if the collector is still processing the previous one. Each dropped
is a word fragment that disappears from the output. It is appropriate for sensor readings or location updates where only the latest matters:
.collectLatest — cancel and restart on new emission
cancels the in-progress collect block the moment a new value arrives. If each token triggers a new emission, the prior block is cancelled before
has time to run, resulting in an empty output. It is useful for debounce patterns where only the final value of a fast sequence needs processing:
When to Use a Raw Channel

) is appropriate when multiple independent producer coroutines need to funnel events into a single consumer. A common case is fan-out logging: one coroutine producing Claude tokens, another producing analytics events, and a single consumer writing both to a log file:
: multiple concurrent producers writing into a shared queue, with one consumer draining it sequentially.

SharedFlow: Converting a Cold Flow to Hot

starts a new SSE connection for every collector. If you need two independent parts of the UI to observe the same Claude stream simultaneously — say, the chat text area and a token-count badge — collecting twice would open two connections.
converts the cold Flow into a hot
backed by a single upstream subscription:
starts the upstream (opens the SSE connection) when the first collector subscribes, and cancels it the moment the last collector unsubscribes. This means:
No SSE connection is open when the user has navigated away (screen is off, Activity is in back stack). The
means no grace period — adjust to e.g.
if you want to keep the connection alive briefly across configuration changes.
means late collectors do not see previously emitted tokens. If you want late subscribers to catch up to the current partial response, set
— but this buffers the entire response in memory.
For most Claude chat UIs, sharing is unnecessary: the ViewModel holds the accumulated
and all composables collect that. Only reach for
when you genuinely have two independent consumers of the raw event stream.
is NOT automatically closed when its owning scope cancels. You must close it explicitly, or the consumer coroutine (waiting on
) will hang indefinitely. This is the main lifecycle hazard with channels compared to Flow:

or manual close management when using the Flow API.

, the OkHttp EventSource keeps running after the collector cancels. The SSE connection stays open, consuming network and producing events that are written to a closed channel (silently ignored but wasteful).

Both strategies drop or cancel intermediate values. For text accumulation every token must be processed in order. Use the default sequential mode.

is set, thousands of token strings accumulate in the channel's internal list, causing unbounded memory growth.

Default sequential Flow backpressure is correct for token accumulation:
suspends until the collect block finishes, guaranteeing in-order delivery without dropping.
